speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is a sister [POINTING] of, of Mayor La Guardia of New York. She, she went to Budapest and married a Jew in the '20s. And then, of course, when Hungary, Budapest, was, was rounded up and all that, the Jews, they took her also to a concentration camp. And her brother did not want to intervene. He said that if I'm not going to intervene for everybody else, I'm not going to intervene into my sister. She's going to have to wait until the war ends. And I found a booklet, in fact, somewhere-- I don't know where that is. She had written a book about her own experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she survived? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: She did survive. But of course, she is-- she was then-- going back to God knows how long. In 1961, she was already in her, I think, late 70s. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These are-- this is Bergen-Belsen. This is the commandant of, of Bergen-Belsen, Josef Kramer. The British captured him, and he was tried, convicted, and executed. These are, these are former SS who were ordered to clean up the, the, the camp. And of course, the dead bodies were all over, all over, as you can see there. You can see the dead bodies here, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] right here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There's a little boy unaffected, apparently, or at least walking as if, as if those dead bodies were not even near him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was in Mauthausen-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nordhausen, I'm sorry, Nordhausen. And this is somewhere in Germany near the Belsen, Bergen-Belsen camp. And likewise, this I believe is also-- this may even be Bergen-Belsen as well. Right here, you can see. Bergen-Belsen was very well [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] publicized in the United States. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Here already, as you can see, are the, the perpetrators who were tried-- these are Nuremberg trials-- tried, convicted, and then they hung them. These are pictures of them. This picture was taken in Buchenwald. I just came across this picture about 10 years ago from a book. And if you go right down the line, right here, that's me, right here. I was recovering from typhus, still. As you can see, I'm very pale. And this was a, a Saturday night service, I believe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of the survivors of Buchenwald. They had little boys [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and little girls, too, in there. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] This [POINTING] I, I did-- first I gathered the coal from the very coal mines where my father and my brother were, Jawarzno concentration, Konzentrationslager. And I decided to put their photographs in there. And that's myself also in there. But it's-- the main thing is that it's original from the subcamp of Auschwitz known as Jawarzno. These are, these are photographs and copies of arts from Theresienstadt. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there are-- they're quite a few of them, quite a few more. But this one I really put out because-- this one in particular because his name happens to be Murmelstein-- Dr. M-U-R. Has nothing-- no relation to me. But he was the eldest, the, the elder, the Terezin elder. And then these are also items, of course, that I brought back with me and then decided to turn them into, yes, into a set of collages. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the mass executions. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And here is some photographs that were taken by the Nazis themselves as their, their killers were leading innocent men, women, and children, of course Jews, in this case, to the woods to, to be, to be executed. And that too, that's a mass execution. It's not sufficient that one SS man shoot him, but they decided that two ought to do it. These are some, some of the rings, wedding rings that the, the SS took or ordered them to give up, you know. The victims gave up their earrings, their rings and other gold items, precious items. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I put this together myself from various photographs, and it's the prisoner pointing a finger at them. I accuse you. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This item, it's a shrunken head from Buchenwald, and to this day it is there on display. I saw it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And likewise, tattooed skin. Ilse Koch was the wife of the commandant of Buchenwald, and this was a hobby. She would select prisoners to be tattooed and then have them shot and then skin 'em and make lampshades out of them. Yeah, this is a demonstration right after the camp was liberated of what punishments were meted out to the prisoners. And here you can see Eisenhower. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is Patton, General Patton and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] General Clark [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and some MPs in the background just watching. This is an interesting picture as well, because the GIs who probably helped liberate-- probably this is Dachau, I have a feeling, or it may very well be another camp that had all these ovens. And yeah, Dachau, I think this is from Dachau,  [BACKGROUND NOISE] because that picture is also Dachau. Dachau did have a gas chambers. But apparently, we're told that it has not been either used extensively or perhaps not used at all. I don't know. But I do know one thing, that this is a demonstration of where the Zyklon B was poured in, where the gas came from to gas the victims. This is one of the camps, and I believe it's-- it may very well be Buchenwald, that the civilian population were ordered to come into the camps. It was right next to them, and they claimed that they didn't know it existed. So [BACKGROUND NOISE] the military authorities wanted to prove to them that it's here. And don't you forget it, is the way they put it, principally. And there-- this is Bergen-Belsen, without a doubt, because I do have footage, a, a movie. It's like a home movie of bulldozers actually bulldozing the bodies into the mass graves. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] These are starving prisoners. So this is how we were liberated. I can picture myself like him or with him there when I was liberated April 11th, 1945 in Buchenwald. This is a, a jacket that I was able to obtain. It's a winter jacket that the prisoners were issued when it got a little cold. This is an original lamp from the, from the, from, from the camps. And I put it together that way, and-- Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, here, they were captured-- these are captured SS, former SS personnel-- ladies, if you will, some of them. And this one was tried, convicted, and executed as well. She was hung. Her name, apparently, is Grese, is the way she was known.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was the commander of the C lager.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was the commandant of--

speaker: interviewer
text: C lager in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Birkenau C lager. Yeah, this is the letter to the editors that I mentioned earlier, about offering the, the reward to prove that Jews were gassed in gas chambers at Auschwitz. And this is the letter that they sent me. If you note, it says personal on it. And they wrote it to me, and they said, in the circumstances, we will reopen the $50,000 reward so that you can apply. See, that, that, that's the key, right there. And I had no choice but to defend myself. And then this is the stipulated judgment. Here is the, here is the court's judicial notice-- 'under evidence of section 452H, this court does take judicial notice of the fact that Jews were gassed in gas chambers at Auschwitz, Poland, and that the Holocaust is not reasonably subject to dispute. It is capable of immediate and accurate determination by resources of indisputable accuracy. It is simply a fact.' And then when we go in here, later on, July the 31st, here is the first check, $50,000 made out to me. And it was hand-carried by them. Here's a second one 30 days later, August the 29th, also hand-carried. There is a third one for $20,000, and that's the last one. And here is the additional $38.36 for the late charges.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's some other court documents. But there are so many that you can be here till midnight, and you might as well just go through them.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a copy of what is in Auschwitz. And here you see Mermelstein, Bernard. That's my father. This is my brother, Lajos, and this is myself in there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This, this is-- these are documents, photocopy of the documents when I arrived in Buchenwald, and it gives the dates and the details about it. And then, of course, is this picture when we were liberated, liberated April the 11th, 1945. And I said to myself, on this picture, as I recognized myself right in there. Then I had it blown up. Right here you can tell a little better that it's me. But I had it verified. And here, April the 29th, 1945, this very same photograph was published in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mel, for showing us the exhibit, but maybe we should finish this tape on happier memories, and so maybe we will show your family pictures this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, sure. That picture was taken in 1920, and those are my grandparents and my aunts and uncles and also my mother, when she was not even married yet at that time. And the photograph was found here. My uncle, that's the one in the hat right there, the only one in that hat, he had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Adolph, Uncle Adolph.

speaker: interviewer
text: His last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Klein. Because my mother's maiden name was Klein. Now, to the left from that person is my mother, and of course, her name was Fany. And then to the left of her, above there, is my aunt. Her name was Ethel. The one to the left was, was, was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Shadi and then-- I, I-- right now it's very difficult to name them so quickly. There is my grandmother holding the youngest child, Chanala.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chanala, yes. Bleema was my, my, grandmother's name. And then the boy was Yoska or Josef Leib. Then my grandfather, of course his name was Mordechai. And then that's another aunt of mine. I'm trying to remember which one it is. But then the one, the boy above is Milton. He is the one that passed away. He died. He came to this country, and he died when he was only 48 years old. And then the one with that cap is Mattheus or Matiash. And he lived in Uruguay. So that's about the extent-- that's my mother's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Munkacs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a, a photograph also taken at Munkacs, probably, I would say, before-- I would say around 1920 as well, because there is my grandmother-- because she passed away, oh, I would say a few years after. And I didn't even know her, to the extent. And there's my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my grandfather's name was Jacob. And the person that came here to visit from America is the one right above there. Her name-- oh, golly, she was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the boy-- yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Last names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther Heller. Well, their name was Mermelstein. See, that's my father's side. My father is not there. These are my aunts and uncles. The one all the way to the extreme left, Zisi, she just died about four, four, five years ago. She died in Israel. And then the others in the back there, the one--

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dvoria Leibe-- you know, you know, it's difficult to, to, to name them, because Dvoria Leibe now, she apparently was taken to the Ukraine, going back to 1941, I believe-- '41 or early '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the young man?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they are-- Geize or Gezele, Geize. And he also, as a matter of fact, he was brought to Auschwitz for-- to the gas chambers. And there are some records. I found records in Auschwitz about him. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to him is Esther. She came from the United States. And then the one after her, her is Rifka.

speaker: interviewer
text: All Mermelsteins?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Mermelstein, yes. Now, the one on the right-hand side, the one in the hat, he is 86 years old now. He lives in Israel-- Moishe Arden, or Moishe.

speaker: interviewer
text: The little boy in the center?

speaker: interviewee
text: The little boy in the center is the son of Esther. And, and, and their name is, is, is, is not Mermelstein, because they were married-- Heller.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Here [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's my father. Of course, he was born in 1899. And he died in Jawarzno. But that picture was taken, I would say, approximately in the year 1941 or '42. So he was about 40 or 41 years old, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: His full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His full name is Bernat Mermelstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was-- where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken, I would say, approximately 1940 or '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munkacs. All those pictures were taken in Munkacs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Munkacs was at that time--

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, part of Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that sign, and, and remember, first and last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- yeah, that-- that-- Yeah, Fany Mermelstein. And this picture was taken before, probably, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was married, I have a feeling, although not too, too far. I would say probably in 1923 or 1924. And it was taken in Munkacs. And as I said, the date of the photo must have been in the '20s sometime, maybe '21, '22, because my mother was married in 1923. That's my sister, and her first name was Etu Mermelstein. And also the picture was taken in Munkacs. I would say that picture was probably taken when she was about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 18-- 16, 17, or 18 years old, which would have been somewhere around the 1940s, early 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my kid sister, Magda Mermelstein. And the picture was also taken in Munkacs, and-- which was part of Hungary at that time. And I, I believe she was approximately 13 or 14 years old there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, that's my brother, Lajos Mermelstein. And I know quite well when that picture was taken. It couldn't have been too far after 1939, January the 6th, because there was a war ongoing there between the Czechs and the Hungarians. And that is a memorial for the Hungarians who died in a battle to try to drive the Czechs away from Munkacs. And the picture was taken in Munkacs. This picture was taken in 1945, I would say in July, the end of July, on our way home from Buchenwald. And I believe the, the, the train stopped in Budapest. And there you can see way above there, the person holding the flag is me, myself, Mel Mermelstein. Yeah, that's me at Fort Mead, Maryland, in the American Army during the Korean War, when I was drafted and trained for battle, I suppose, for getting ready to Korea, if you will. But I never did go to Korea. But this picture is a, a, a memorable picture for me. And I don't want to elaborate on it, but it was good memories and some very, very strange memories, because in the background, as you can see, barracks. And barracks are very, very related to concentration camps. But the date of the photo, as I said, was 1950. I would say October-- yes, or November, because see, we already are wearing heavier clothing, so it was probably around that time, yes. OK, in the very front is my wife, Jane Mermelstein. And then the one holding his arms around is Berny, the oldest son, Berny Mermelstein. To his-- or to my right is David Mermelstein. And then going back left, the other boy all the way to the extreme left is my son Kenny. And then in the, in the background all the way in the back is Edie. Berny-- this picture was taken, I would say, about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] four, five years ago, OK, four, five years ago. And Berny is 33, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Edie is 31, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Kenny is 29, and David is 24.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken-- I believe this was taken somewhere on a journey, on a, on a vacation, and I don't remember exactly where.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: About four years ago. I'd say around 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Aren't you going to show the other one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this picture was taken, I would say, about three or four years ago. And it is Michael, yes. Michael is our only grandson, and he is now six years old, of course. And this picture was taken at his grandmother's home back in the South, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? In Tennessee. This is on a farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: In, in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1991, I believe it is, or--
